era_context: Classical Greece in the early Classical period. Athens is at the height of its imperial power, controlling much of the Aegean through the Delian League and dominating central Greece militarily. The First Peloponnesian War (461–446 BCE) is in its early stages. Pericles is consolidating political power following Ephialtes' death. The Egyptian campaign is in its final stages before collapse. Sparta remains the dominant land power but is increasingly challenged by Athens. Egypt is under Persian control with ongoing rebellion. China's Spring and Autumn period continues.

events.title: Assassination of Ephialtes (alternative dating)
events.description: Ephialtes, the radical democratic reformer, is assassinated by conservative opponents. This event marks a turning point in Athenian politics, removing a key democratic leader and consolidating Pericles' political dominance. The assassination reflects the bitter factional struggles within Athens over the pace and scope of democratic reform.

sources.name: Aristotle, Constitution of the Athenians, Chapter 25

events.certainty_note: Ephialtes' assassination is attested in later sources, but the precise date is uncertain. Scholars place it between 460 and 456 BCE. The assignment to 457 BCE is one of several conventional datings. Some scholars prefer 458 or 456 BCE.

events.title: Athenian construction of the Long Walls begins
events.description: Athens begins construction of the Long Walls, a massive fortification project that will connect Athens to its port at Piraeus. The walls are designed to protect the city's supply lines and ensure that Athens can maintain its naval power even if surrounded by land forces. The project is a major engineering undertaking and reflects Athenian confidence in its naval dominance and wealth. Construction will continue for several years.
key_figures: Athenian architects and engineers (names not preserved)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.107
Archaeological evidence from the Long Walls

events.certainty_note: The construction of the Long Walls is well-attested in literary sources and archaeological evidence. The dating to 457 BCE is conventional, though some scholars place the beginning of construction in the 450s BCE. The project was completed in stages over several decades.
cross_references: 431-001 (role of Long Walls in Peloponnesian War)

events.title: Athenian naval operations in the Aegean and consolidation of Delian League
events.description: Athenian naval forces continue operations throughout the Aegean, consolidating control over the Delian League and suppressing any attempts at secession. These operations are less dramatic than the Egyptian campaign but represent the steady expansion of Athenian naval hegemony and the transformation of the league into an Athenian empire.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98–108

events.certainty_note: Thucydides attests to ongoing Athenian naval activity in this period but does not provide year-by-year detail. The assignment to 457 BCE is conventional.

events.title: Egyptian campaign faces mounting Persian pressure
events.description: The Athenian expedition to Egypt, now in its fourth year, faces severe Persian military pressure. The campaign is becoming increasingly costly and politically contentious in Athens. Persian forces under Artaxerxes I are mobilizing to crush the rebellion and expel the Athenian forces. The campaign will collapse within a few years, resulting in the loss of much of the Athenian fleet.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.104

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the campaign is inferred from the broader chronology. No specific events are recorded for 457 BCE.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Ephialtes' assassination is highly uncertain and is variously placed in 460, 458, 457, or 456 BCE by different scholars. The dating of the Long Walls' construction is also debated; some scholars place the beginning in the 450s BCE rather than 457 BCE. The significance of the Long Walls as a strategic innovation is accepted, but their exact role in Athenian strategy is debated.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 457 BCE is sparse and largely reconstructed from Thucydides' summary narrative and later compilations. No contemporary Athenian inscriptions or documents are known to survive for this specific year. The Egyptian perspective is almost entirely absent from Greek sources. The dating of events in this period is conventional and subject to significant scholarly debate. The sources are biased toward Athenian perspectives.

graph_edges.note: Ephialtes' assassination follows from his democratic reforms
The Long Walls become crucial to Athenian strategy in the Peloponnesian War
The Egyptian campaign continues toward its eventual failure
